pattern_recognition: Graph Theory - This problem can be recognized as a graph traversal problem where courses are nodes and prerequisites are directed edges. The presence of cycles in this directed graph indicates that not all courses can be completed.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Try all possible orders of taking courses and check if the prerequisites are satisfied.
approaches.algorithm: Generate all permutations of course orders and validate each against the prerequisites.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use topological sorting to determine if a valid order exists that satisfies all prerequisites.
approaches.algorithm: Construct a directed graph from the prerequisites and perform a topological sort using Kahn's algorithm or DFS.

dry_run: For numCourses = 4 and prerequisites = [[1,0],[2,1],[3,2]], we can represent this as a directed graph: 0 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3. Starting from course 0, we can take course 1 next, then course 2, and finally course 3. Since we can complete all courses in this order, the output is true.
edge_cases: If numCourses is 0, return true as there are no courses to complete. If there is only one course with no prerequisites, return true. If there are duplicate prerequisites, they should be ignored. For large inputs, ensure the algorithm handles the graph efficiently to avoid performance issues.
common_pitfalls: 1) Failing to detect cycles in the graph can lead to incorrect conclusions about course completion. 2) Not handling cases where there are no prerequisites correctly can lead to false negatives. 3) Misunderstanding the input format (e.g., treating prerequisites as undirected edges) can lead to incorrect graph construction.
